      Programming note: Money Stuff will be off tomorrow, back on Wednesday=
.SPAC-downOne big advantage of going public by merging with a special purpo=
se acquisition company, instead of through a traditional initial public off=
ering, is that the SPAC tends to offer more certainty of price and size. Wi=
th an IPO, you announce =E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=99d like to raise $400 million at=
 a valuation of $1.8 billion to $2.2 billion,=E2=80=9D or whatever, and the=
n you go out and market the deal to investors. Perhaps they love it and you=
 upsize and reprice the deal, raising $500 million at a $2.5 billion valuat=
ion. Perhaps they hate it and you downsize and reprice to raise $300 millio=
n at a $1.5 billion valuation. Perhaps they super hate it and you end up pu=
lling the deal, raising no money at any valuation. Most IPOs mostly go fine=
, but some go poorly, and if yours goes poorly that=E2=80=99s a serious bla=
ck eye.With a SPAC,  you negotiate the price=C2=A0first. You meet with SPAC=
s, and you discuss valuation, and you agree to a deal at a specific valuati=
on. Alongside that deal, you negotiate a PIPE, a private investment in publ=
ic equity, with several big institutional investors. Then when the price an=
d size are locked up, you announce the deal. =E2=80=9CWe=E2=80=99re going t=
o raise $455 million at a $1.9 billion valuation,=E2=80=9D you say, and you=
 have the signed merger agreement to prove it. By the time you go public wi=
th the deal, the deal is done; the risk of failure and embarrassment is min=
imized.I mean, roughly. Actually there are contingencies to the SPAC too. I=
n particular, the shareholders of the SPAC =E2=80=94=C2=A0the investors who=
 have put money into the SPAC=E2=80=99s pot of money, hoping that it will f=
ind and negotiate a good acquisition =E2=80=94=C2=A0get to evaluate the dea=
l after you announce it. If they don=E2=80=99t like it, they can vote again=
st it. Or they can pull their money out of the SPAC: They invested $10 per =
share in the SPAC, and it was put into a trust; if they don=E2=80=99t like =
the deal that the SPAC does, they can get their money back from the trust w=
ith interest. If your deal was for $455 million, with $230 million from the=
 SPAC=E2=80=99s trust and $225 million from the PIPE, that $230 million is =
fully at risk: You might end up with $225 million, or $455 million, or anyt=
hing in between. Or the SPAC shareholders might vote no and the whole deal =
will be off.=C2=A0So there is still marketing to be done =E2=80=94=C2=A0to =
the SPAC=E2=80=99s shareholders =E2=80=94=C2=A0after the deal is announced.=
 And this marketing takes a bit of time; you have to do a proxy statement a=
nd get a shareholder vote and get regulatory approvals and so forth. And=C2=
=A0if anything goes wrong in that time =E2=80=94=C2=A0if there's bad news a=
bout the company, or if the market goes down =E2=80=94=C2=A0then you are at=
 risk for the same sort of embarrassing black eye as you=E2=80=99d get from=
 a failed IPO.As with IPOs, this is not much of a problem for hot deals in =
booming markets: You announce the deal, the SPAC=E2=80=99s shares =E2=80=94=
 which represent $10 in cash =E2=80=94=C2=A0trade up from, say, $12 (where =
they were trading based on the mere=C2=A0hope=C2=A0of a hot deal) to, say, =
$20 (reflecting a view that the SPAC is dramatically underpaying for the co=
mpany it is taking public), everybody votes in favor of the deal, nobody re=
deems, everything goes great and I  write a smug post about how you =E2=80=
=9Cleft money on the table=E2=80=9D by selling $20 worth of stock for $10.=
=C2=A0But as with IPOs, as the market cools, sometimes deals struggle. Here=
=E2=80=99s one:CF Finance Acquisition Corp. III (Nasdaq: CFAC) (=E2=80=9CCF=
 III=E2=80=9D), a special purpose acquisition company sponsored by Cantor F=
itzgerald, and AEye, Inc. (=E2=80=9CAEye=E2=80=9D), the global leader in ac=
tive, next generation LiDAR solutions, today announced that due to recent v=
aluation changes of publicly traded lidar companies and changing conditions=
 in the automotive lidar industry, they have amended their previously annou=
nced merger agreement. Under the terms of the amended merger agreement, AEy=
e will be valued on a pre-merger basis at $1.52 billion at the closing of t=
he transaction, compared to $1.9 billion at the time of the merger announce=
ment in February 2021. =E2=80=A6Assuming no redemptions by CF III stockhold=
ers, gross proceeds from the transaction are expected to be up to $455 mill=
ion, including the contribution of up to $230 million of cash held in CF II=
I=E2=80=99s trust account. The transaction is further supported by a $225 m=
illion fully committed PIPE anchored by strategic and institutional investo=
rs including Continental, GM Ventures, Subaru-SBI, Intel Capital, Hella Ven=
tures, and Taiwania Capital.Here are the original merger agreement=C2=A0and=
 press release, from February, and today=E2=80=99s amendment. As far as I c=
an tell both the SPAC shareholders and the PIPE investors will get the new =
lower price; they agreed to $1.9 billion back in February but the market ha=
s changed and they=E2=80=99re not willing to do that anymore. And they have=
 leverage to renegotiate, because if the price is too high, the SPAC shareh=
olders will just vote it down. CF Finance III closed at $9.98 on Friday, be=
low its trust value, suggesting that most shareholders would choose to rede=
em; this morning it traded a bit north of $10, suggesting that at the new p=
rice there=E2=80=99s a better shot of getting the deal done.=C2=A0I assume =
there will be more of this. In hot markets, most IPOs trade above their IPO=
 price, and most SPACs trade above their deal price.[1] In cooler markets, =
some IPOs reprice down and still struggle. The same should be true of SPACs=
.SPACs are weird though because of a time lag: In hot SPAC markets (like th=
e end of 2020 and the beginning of 2021), lots of sponsors go out and raise=
 lots of money for lots of SPACs. Then the money sits in the SPACs=E2=80=99=
 pots as they look for companies to take public. For the sponsors to make m=
oney, they need to find a deal and get it approved. That means a lot of SPA=
Cs chasing deals even as the market cools and SPAC shareholders, and potent=
ial PIPE investors, get increasingly picky. That might mean a lot of repric=
ing.Elsewhere:Of the 41 special purpose acquisition companies that have com=
pleted transactions since the start of 2020, only three are even within 5 p=
er cent of their share price peaks. Eighteen of them have more than halved,=
 and several are down by more than 80 per cent. The average decline is 39 p=
er cent.Archegos feesWe  talked last month about the odd fact that Credit S=
uisse=E2=80=99s chief executive officer and chief risk officer =E2=80=9Conl=
y became aware of the bank=E2=80=99s exposure to Archegos in the days leadi=
ng up to the forced liquidation of the fund.=E2=80=9D Archegos Capital Mana=
gement  is of course the family office of Bill Hwang, which accumulated eno=
rmous concentrated levered=C2=A0positions in a handful of stocks and then m=
issed a margin call. Its bank counterparties sold off the stocks =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0some more quickly than others =E2=80=94=C2=A0and the ones who waited =
too long, particularly Credit Suisse, got smoked. Credit Suisse had more th=
an $20 billion of Archegos exposure at the peak, and lost $5.4 billion in t=

Should index funds be illegal?There is  a theory, which we  talk about a lo=
t around here, that says that when a bunch of companies in the same industr=
y have the same shareholders =E2=80=94=C2=A0when they are all owned by the =
same handful of big asset managers =E2=80=94=C2=A0that will reduce competit=
ion in product markets. If all the airlines are owned by the same group of =
index funds, those overlapping shareholders will not want any one airline t=
o cut prices to win market share, because that market share will come at th=
e expense of the other airlines that the shareholders also own.  The theory=
 suggests that the airlines will consider these interests of their common s=
hareholders, and compete less vigorously.I=C2=A0have suggested that this is=
 actually just one application of a much more general theory. I wrote last =
February:The structure of the argument has such wide application. If you be=
lieve =E2=80=A6=C2=A0that common ownership of multiple companies by big ins=
titutional investors can somehow cause them all to act like they=E2=80=99re=
 on the same team, then you can believe it about anything. You can talk abo=
ut it in terms of consumer prices, which is what started all of the worryin=
g about common ownership: If all the airlines are owned by the same funds, =
won=E2=80=99t they raise airfares, etc. You can talk about it in terms of t=
ax evasion, apparently. But really anything that would be hard for a compan=
y to do individually, but that would be good for companies collectively, ca=
n fit into this story.I listed some possible examples, good and bad. An obv=
ious good one that I mentioned is that climate change raises a host of coll=
ective action problems that can perhaps be addressed by companies with the =
same owners. Since then, the Covid-19 pandemic has supplied another good ex=
ample, and we have  talked a few  times about the  notion that, when pharma=
ceutical companies are owned by the same big index funds that own every oth=
er company, they have pro-social incentives to cooperate to find vaccines a=
nd distribute them cheaply: The vaccine is good for the economy, which is g=
ood for every company, which is good for the bottom line of the pharmaceuti=
cal companies=E2=80=99=C2=A0shareholders=C2=A0whether or not it=E2=80=99s g=
ood for the individual bottom lines of the individual pharmaceutical compan=
ies.=C2=A0An obvious bad one, I wrote, was labor-market competition:Here=E2=
=80=99s one: =E2=80=9CCommon ownership depresses employee wages: If one com=
pany cuts wages it will lose skilled workers to competitors, but=C2=A0if th=
ey all agree to cut wages the workers will have no ability to push back, an=
d index funds blah blah blah.=E2=80=9D That=E2=80=99s sort of an obvious ex=
tension of the antitrust theory. I have not Googled it carefully but I assu=
me that there is already a literature; if there isn=E2=80=99t, though, go w=
rite it!=C2=A0That=E2=80=99ll get you tenure!=C2=A0Real wage stagnation=C2=
=A0over the past few decades has coincided with the rise of index funds and=
=C2=A0common ownership, so, you know, it=C2=A0feels=C2=A0empirically true. =
(You=E2=80=99ll probably want to be more careful empirically, for tenure.)=
=C2=A0And the theory behind it is the same as the theory behind everything =
else.=C2=A0Well,=C2=A0now=C2=A0there=E2=80=99s a literature. Here is =E2=80=
=9CCommon Ownership and the Decline of the American Worker,=E2=80=9D by Zoh=
ar Goshen and Doron Levit:The last forty years have seen two major economic=
 trends: Wages have stalled despite rising productivity, and institutional =
investors have replaced retail shareholders as the predominant owners of th=
e American equity markets. A few powerful institutional investors=E2=80=94d=
ubbed common owners=E2=80=94now hold large stakes in most U.S. corporations=
. It is not a coincidence that at the same time American workers got a new =
set of bosses, their wages stopped growing, and shareholder returns went up=

policy of pushing public corporations to adopt strong corporate governance =
has an inherent, painful tradeoff. While strong governance can improve corp=
orate efficiency=E2=80=94by reducing management agency costs=E2=80=94it can=
 also reduce social welfare=E2=80=94by limiting investment and depressing t=
he labor market. The shift to strong governance causes managers to limit in=

ng their size.I guess it is not an empirical literature. You can imagine a =

mpany, and good governance =E2=80=94=C2=A0in the classic corporate-governan=

=C2=A0leads to reduced investment and hiring.=C2=A0=C2=A0Again, it=E2=80=99=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Programming note: </strong><em>Money S=
tuff will be off tomorrow, back on Wednesday.</em></p><h2 style=3D"font-wei=
ght: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial, Helvetica, sans-ser=
if; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bottom-width: 1px; border=
-bottom-style: dotted; padding-bottom: 5px; margin-top: 30px; margin-bottom=
: 0;">SPAC-down</h2><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One big advantage of going=
 public by merging with a special purpose acquisition company, instead of t=
hrough a traditional initial public offering, is that the SPAC tends to off=
er <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/23728147.287998=
/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL25ld3NsZXR0ZXJzLzIwMjAtMDYtMjMvbW=
9uZXktc3R1ZmYtYmlsbC1hY2ttYW4td2FudHMtYS1tYXR1cmUtdW5pY29ybg/607f07ceef4b85=
24a319a23cB71c7baaf" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;">more certainty of price and size</a>. With an IPO, you announc=
e =E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=99d like to raise $400 million at a valuation of $1.8 b=
illion to $2.2 billion,=E2=80=9D or whatever, and then you go out and marke=
t the deal to investors. Perhaps they love it and you upsize and reprice th=
e deal, raising $500 million at a $2.5 billion valuation. Perhaps they hate=
 it and you downsize and reprice to raise $300 million at a $1.5 billion va=
luation. Perhaps they super hate it and you end up pulling the deal, raisin=
g no money at any valuation. Most IPOs mostly go fine, but some go poorly, =
and if yours goes poorly that=E2=80=99s a serious black eye.</p><p style=3D=
"margin: 16px 0;">With a SPAC, <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusine=
ss.com/click/23728147.287998/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2=
FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDMtMjMvaGFwcHktZ2FtZXN0b3AtZWFybmluZ3MtZGF5/607f07ceef4b=
8524a319a23cBb0ad233d" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;"> you negotiate the price=C2=A0<em>first</em></a>. You meet wit=
h SPACs, and you discuss valuation, and you agree to a deal at a specific v=
aluation. Alongside that deal, you negotiate a PIPE, a private investment i=
n public equity, with several big institutional investors. Then when the pr=
ice and size are locked up, you announce the deal. =E2=80=9CWe=E2=80=99re g=
oing to raise $455 million at a $1.9 billion valuation,=E2=80=9D you say, a=
nd you have the signed merger agreement to prove it. By the time you go pub=
lic with the deal, the deal is done; the risk of failure and embarrassment =
is minimized.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I mean, roughly. Actually the=
re are contingencies to the SPAC too. In particular, the shareholders of th=
e SPAC =E2=80=94=C2=A0the investors who have put money into the SPAC=E2=80=
=99s pot of money, hoping that it will find and negotiate a good acquisitio=
n =E2=80=94=C2=A0get to evaluate the deal after you announce it. If they do=
n=E2=80=99t like it, they can vote against it. Or they can <a href=3D"https=
://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/23728147.287998/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYm=
xvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL25ld3NsZXR0ZXJzLzIwMjEtMDEtMDgvbW9uZXktc3R1ZmYtc3BhY=
y1tYWdpYy1pc24tdC1mcmVl/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB883f42cc" itemprop=3D"Stor=
yLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: no=
ne !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bord=
er-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">pull their money out of th=
e SPAC</a>: They invested $10 per share in the SPAC, and it was put into a =
trust; if they don=E2=80=99t like the deal that the SPAC does, they can get=
 their money back from the trust with interest. If your deal was for $455 m=
illion, with $230 million from the SPAC=E2=80=99s trust and $225 million fr=
om the PIPE, that $230 million is fully at risk: You might end up with $225=
 million, or $455 million, or anything in between. Or the SPAC shareholders=
 might vote no and the whole deal will be off.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:=
 16px 0;">So there is still marketing to be done =E2=80=94=C2=A0to the SPAC=
=E2=80=99s shareholders =E2=80=94=C2=A0after the deal is announced. And thi=
s marketing takes a bit of time; you have to do a proxy statement and get a=
 shareholder vote and get regulatory approvals and so forth. And=C2=A0if an=
ything goes wrong in that time =E2=80=94=C2=A0if there's bad news about the=
 company, or if the market goes down =E2=80=94=C2=A0then you are at risk fo=
r the same sort of embarrassing black eye as you=E2=80=99d get from a faile=
d IPO.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">As with IPOs, this is not much of a =
problem for hot deals in booming markets: You announce the deal, the SPAC=
=E2=80=99s shares =E2=80=94 which represent $10 in cash =E2=80=94=C2=A0trad=
e up from, say, $12 (where they were trading based on the mere=C2=A0<em>hop=
e=C2=A0</em>of a hot deal) to, say, $20 (reflecting a view that the SPAC is=
 dramatically underpaying for the company it is taking public), everybody v=
otes in favor of the deal, nobody redeems, everything goes great and I <a h=
ref=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/23728147.287998/aHR0cH=
M6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDItMjMvdGVzbGEtc=
ml2YWwtbHVjaWQtbW90b3JzLXBhaWQtYS1wcmljZS1mb3ItaXRzLXNwYWMtZGVhbA/607f07cee=
f4b8524a319a23cB65eab9a3" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" st=
yle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-widt=
h: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgroun=
d-color: #ccc;"> write a smug post</a> about how you =E2=80=9Cleft money on=
 the table=E2=80=9D by selling $20 worth of stock for $10.=C2=A0</p><p styl=
e=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But as with IPOs, as the market cools, sometimes deal=
s struggle. <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/237281=
47.287998/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE4MTg2NDQvM=
DAwMTIxMzkwMDIxMDI0MTE3L2VhMTQwMjI4ZXg5OS0xX2NmZmluYW5jZTMuaHRt/607f07ceef4=
b8524a319a23cB324c3709" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" styl=
e=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width:=
 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-=
color: #ccc;">Here=E2=80=99s one</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16=
px 0;">CF Finance Acquisition Corp. III (Nasdaq: CFAC) (=E2=80=9CCF III=E2=
=80=9D), a special purpose acquisition company sponsored by Cantor Fitzgera=
ld, and AEye, Inc. (=E2=80=9CAEye=E2=80=9D), the global leader in active, n=
ext generation LiDAR solutions, today announced that due to recent valuatio=
n changes of publicly traded lidar companies and changing conditions in the=
 automotive lidar industry, they have amended their previously announced me=
rger agreement. Under the terms of the amended merger agreement, AEye will =
be valued on a pre-merger basis at $1.52 billion at the closing of the tran=
saction, compared to $1.9 billion at the time of the merger announcement in=
 February 2021. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Assuming no redem=
ptions by CF III stockholders, gross proceeds from the transaction are expe=
cted to be up to $455 million, including the contribution of up to $230 mil=
lion of cash held in CF III=E2=80=99s trust account. The transaction is fur=
ther supported by a $225 million fully committed PIPE anchored by strategic=
 and institutional investors including Continental, GM Ventures, Subaru-SBI=
, Intel Capital, Hella Ventures, and Taiwania Capital.</p></blockquote><p s=
tyle=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here are the <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomber=
gbusiness.com/click/23728147.287998/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9=
lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE4MTg2NDQvMDAwMTIxMzkwMDIxMDA5OTQzL2VhMTM1ODUyZXgyLTFfY2ZmaW=
5hbmNlMy5odG0/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBd49f9dda" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" ite=
mscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !import=
ant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-=
style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">original merger agreement</a>=C2=A0a=
nd <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/23728147.287998=
/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE4MTg2NDQvMDAwMTIxMz=
kwMDIxMDA5OTQzL2VhMTM1ODUyZXg5OS0xX2NmZmluYW5jZTMuaHRt/607f07ceef4b8524a319=
a23cB6742a7d1" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"colo=
r: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bor=
der-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #c=
cc;">press release</a>, from February, and <a href=3D"https://link.mail.blo=
ombergbusiness.com/click/23728147.287998/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaX=
Zlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzE4MTg2NDQvMDAwMTIxMzkwMDIxMDI0MTE3L2VhMTQwMjI4ZXgyLTFfY=
2ZmaW5hbmNlMy5odG0/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBa760b35f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink=
" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !i=
mportant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bo=
ttom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">today=E2=80=99s amendment</a>. =
As far as I can tell both the SPAC shareholders and the PIPE investors will=
 get the new lower price; they agreed to $1.9 billion back in February but =
the market has changed and they=E2=80=99re not willing to do that anymore. =
And they have leverage to renegotiate, because if the price is too high, th=
e SPAC shareholders will just vote it down. CF Finance III closed at $9.98 =
on Friday, below its trust value, suggesting that most shareholders would c=
hoose to redeem; this morning it traded a bit north of $10, suggesting that=
 at the new price there=E2=80=99s a better shot of getting the deal done.=
=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I assume there will be more of this.=
 In hot markets, most IPOs trade above their IPO price, and most SPACs trad=
e above their deal price.[1] In cooler markets, some IPOs reprice down and =
still struggle. The same should be true of SPACs.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16=
px 0;">SPACs are weird though because of a time lag: In hot SPAC markets (l=
ike the end of 2020 and the beginning of 2021), lots of sponsors go out and=
 raise lots of money for lots of SPACs. Then the money sits in the SPACs=E2=
=80=99 pots as they look for companies to take public. For the sponsors to =
make money, they need to find a deal and get it approved. That means a lot =
of SPACs chasing deals even as the market cools and SPAC shareholders, and =
potential PIPE investors, get increasingly picky. That might mean a lot of =
repricing.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><a href=3D"https://link.mail.blo=
ombergbusiness.com/click/23728147.287998/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZnQuY29tL2NvbnRlbn=
QvYzdjMGYxMWMtYjY3OC00OTFkLTkxNzMtZmU0OWVhMjliNGI4/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23c=
Bcb3b61e2" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #=
000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-=
bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"=
>Elsewhere</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Of the 41 speci=
al purpose acquisition companies that have completed transactions since the=
 start of 2020, only three are even within 5 per cent of their share price =
peaks. Eighteen of them have more than halved, and several are down by more=
 than 80 per cent. The average decline is 39 per cent.</p></blockquote><h2 =
style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial, He=
lvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bottom-w=
idth: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; padding-bottom: 5px; margin-top: 30=
px; margin-bottom: 0;">Archegos fees</h2><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We <a=
 href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/23728147.287998/aHR0=
cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDQtMjIvanBtb3J=
nYW4tcy1ldXJvcGVhbi1zdXBlci1sZWFndWUtZGViYWNsZS1pcy1hYm91dC1zdGFrZWhvbGRlcn=
M/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB543988a8" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"it=
emscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-=
bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid=
; background-color: #ccc;"> talked last month</a> about the odd fact that C=
redit Suisse=E2=80=99s chief executive officer and chief risk officer =E2=
=80=9Conly became aware of the bank=E2=80=99s exposure to Archegos in the d=
ays leading up to the forced liquidation of the fund.=E2=80=9D Archegos Cap=
ital Management <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/23=
728147.287998/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwM=
jEtMDQtMDUvYmlsbC1od2FuZy1zLWFyY2hlZ29zLXN3YXAtYWZ0ZXJtYXRoLWlzLWF3a3dhcmQt=
Zm9yLWJhbmtz/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB0e3d3327" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" item=
scope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !importa=
nt; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-s=
tyle: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> is of course</a> the family office o=
f Bill Hwang, which accumulated enormous concentrated levered=C2=A0position=
s in a handful of stocks and then missed a margin call. Its bank counterpar=
ties sold off the stocks =E2=80=94=C2=A0some more quickly than others =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0and the ones who waited too long, particularly Credit Suisse, g=
ot smoked. Credit Suisse had <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness=
.com/click/23728147.287998/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy9jcmVkaXQt=
c3Vpc3Nlcy1leHBvc3VyZS10by1hcmNoZWdvcy1pbnZlc3RtZW50cy1ncmV3LXRvLW1vcmUtdGh=
hbi0yMC1iaWxsaW9uLTExNjE5MDQ1OTg4/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBfb81ceea" itempr=
op=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"color: #000000; text-deco=
ration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #00=
0000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">more than $20 bi=
llion</a> of Archegos exposure at the peak, and <a href=3D"https://link.mai=
l.bloombergbusiness.com/click/23728147.287998/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZnQuY29tL2Nvb=
nRlbnQvNDI5ZjJjZDItZGI1NS00MmI4LWE2NWMtYTIyOGNkYjMwODlk/607f07ceef4b8524a31=
9a23cBd587cbbf" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" style=3D"col=
or: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bo=
rder-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #=
ccc;">lost $5.4 billion</a> in the crash.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">L=
ast month, I speculated that senior management at Credit Suisse might not h=
ave paid too much attention to Archegos because, when it started, it wasn=
=E2=80=99t a particularly big client: Archegos started relatively small, ma=
de aggressive bets, kept winning, and kept doubling down; its exposures onl=
y became massive pretty recently. But here=E2=80=99s another reason that se=
nior managers might not have paid attention: Revenue-wise, <a href=3D"https=
://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/23728147.287998/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYm=
xvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDQtMjIvanBtb3JnYW4tcy1ldXJvc=
GVhbi1zdXBlci1sZWFndWUtZGViYWNsZS1pcy1hYm91dC1zdGFrZWhvbGRlcnM/607f07ceef4b=
